title: Mount host resolv.conf does not work as expected.
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

This is a follow up of #10026, unfortunately I had no chance to have tried out suggested tips until today and in the meantime the issue has been closed for inactivity. Opening this one as a continuation.

Even if I use `--dns=none` and `-v /etc/resolv.conf:/etc/resolv.conf`, `/etc/resolv.conf` inside the container does not reflect the changes I make to the host's one. Here what I did:

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```bash
host > cat /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 192.168.1.1
```
```bash
host > podman container run --rm -ti --dns=none -v /etc/resolv.conf:/etc/resolv.conf alpine:latest sh
guest > cat /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 192.168.1.1
```
Then I change the `/etc/resolv.conf` in the host.
```bash
host > cat /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 10.160.0.1
nameserver 10.160.2.88
nameserver 2620:113:80c0:8080:10:160:2:88
# NOTE: the libc resolver may not support more than 3 nameservers.
# The nameservers listed below may not be recognized.
nameserver 2620:113:80c0:8080:10:160:0:1
nameserver 192.168.1.1
```
I then check again the file from the container, expecting to see the above changes reflected:
```bash
guest > cat /etc/resolv.conf
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 192.168.1.1
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

API Version:  3.2.2
Go Version:   go1.16.5
Git Commit:   d577c44e359f9f8284b38cf984f939b3020badc3
Built:        Fri Jul  9 14:54:04 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```

comments.body: Do you edit the file? I assume not. I think NetworkManager will create a new `resolv.conf` file for each change and overwrites  `/etc/resolv.conf`. This breaks the bind mount because the source was "deleted". The container keeps using the old file while the host has the new file. This is how bind mounts work in the kernel. One solution would be to mount the parent directory, but this is probably not desired for `/etc`

You are correct, I do not directly edit the file, I connect the host to a VPN via NetworkManager and in turn it changes `/etc/resolv.conf`. I have not thought it might delete the file and replace it with the new one, this actually explains the behavior I experienced. As you anticipated, mounting the whole `/etc` it's out of scope. I'm wondering what happens if I create a symlink to `/etc/resolv.conf` and mount that one instead. It should not be deleted, and after a few milliseconds of being a broken link (while NetworkManager is replacing `resolv.conf`) it will keep pointing to the same destination which is now the updated `resolv.conf`. I'm gonna try this way out soon and I will post here the result.
This will not work. Symlinks are always resolved before doing the bind mount.
Are you aware of any other solution? Maybe I could avoid to bind mount `/etc/resolv.conf`?

Maybe there is a way to prevent NetworkManager from creating a new file and instead edit this in place. Or you setup a local resolver with a fixed ip so you do not have to change /etc/resolv.conf every time.

I close the issue since I do not think this can be fixed in podman.

The solution that Toolbox ended up using was to mount `/etc/` from the host into the container at `/host/etc/` and then symlink `/etc/resolv.conf` in the container to `/host/etc/resolv.conf` - because the entire `/etc/` directory is bind-mounted in, changes are captured.

In [secbox](https://github.com/StayPirate/secbox) I took a different approach, it maintains (syncs) it's own copy of the host's `/etc/resolv.conf` as explained in [this commit](https://github.com/StayPirate/secbox/commit/ce2a46332222a07796624cc9c3466229a1592848). TBH I don't know how toolbox works, but if you use it by spawning a shell and keep working from there, then secbox's solution won't work. Secbox is transparent for the user, commands are ran from the host's terminal and their output is printed there.
